preFix.title: Repairing a dripping compression fitting
preFix.subtitle: Isolate the pipe, dismantle the fitting, replace the olive or washer, and reassemble without leaks
preFix.excerpt: How to stop a leak at a compression fitting on copper or plastic plumbing pipe. Covers identifying which part of the fitting has failed, replacing the olive or insert, and tightening correctly to restore a dry joint.
preFix.sourceNotes: Traditional UK plumbing practice. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: leaks for one of three reasons: the nut was never tight enough, the

has deformed or corroded, or the pipe was not cut square. In most cases a quarter-turn with a spanner fixes it. If the fitting has been over-tightened before, the olive is spent and the fitting needs cutting out.
Step 1, Isolate the supply and drain the pipe

Close the nearest isolating valve or the main stopcock. Open a tap below the leak to drain pressure. Put a towel and a bowl under the fitting, even an isolated pipe holds residual water, and tipping the pipe releases it.

If the leak is at the nut rather than from the body of the fitting, try a firm quarter-turn clockwise with the adjustable spanner while holding the fitting body still with the water-pump pliers. Restore the supply and check. If the leak stops, the job is done. If the nut will not turn or the leak continues, dismantle.
Step 3, Dismantle and inspect the olive

Undo the compression nut anti-clockwise and slide the fitting off the pipe. The olive will be embedded in the pipe, try to slide it off. If it moves freely, it can sometimes be reused if undamaged; if it is cracked, flattened, or stuck, cut the pipe back 50 mm with the pipe cutter and start with a new olive. For plastic pipe, check that the support insert (a brass sleeve inside the pipe end) is still seated, without it the pipe crushes and the joint cannot seal.
Step 4, Reassemble with a new olive

Slide the nut onto the pipe, then the new olive, then push the pipe fully into the fitting body until it bottoms out. Thread the nut on by hand, then tighten with the spanner to firm, one and a quarter turns past hand-tight is the standard for a Type A compression fitting. Do not over-tighten: a burred or cracked olive leaks as readily as a loose one. Restore the supply and inspect the joint for 30 seconds before declaring it dry.

items.fix: Cut the pipe back past the old olive, fit a new olive, and check the pipe end is square before reassembling.
items.cause: Olive is deformed or the pipe is not cut square

items.fix: Remove the fitting, inspect the pipe end, fit or replace the brass insert, then reassemble. Plastic pipe compresses without the insert and cannot form a seal.
items.cause: Support insert missing or displaced inside the pipe end

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
